mcqs.question: What does asymptotic analysis primarily measure?

options.text: How running time grows as input size approaches infinity

mcqs.explanation: Asymptotic analysis measures the growth rate of an algorithm's resource requirements as input size approaches infinity, focusing on the trend rather than exact measurements.

mcqs.question: Which notation represents the worst-case analysis of an algorithm?
mcqs.explanation: Big O notation represents the upper bound, which is used to describe the worst-case scenario - the maximum time or space an algorithm will require.

mcqs.question: If an algorithm has time complexity T(n) = 3n² + 2n + 1, what is its asymptotic complexity?
mcqs.explanation: We ignore lower-order terms and constants. The highest-order term is n², so the complexity is O(n²).

mcqs.question: Which algorithm has O(log n) time complexity?
mcqs.explanation: Binary Search has O(log n) complexity because it halves the search space with each comparison.

mcqs.question: What is the time complexity of a nested loop where the outer loop runs n times and inner loop runs from 1 to n?
mcqs.explanation: The inner loop runs n times for each iteration of the outer loop, giving n × n = n² iterations.

mcqs.question: Which complexity is considered efficient?
mcqs.explanation: O(n log n) is considered efficient as it is close to linear time and is achieved by efficient sorting algorithms like Merge Sort.

mcqs.question: What does Ω(1) represent?
mcqs.explanation: Big Omega (Ω) represents the lower bound, so Ω(1) means the algorithm takes at least constant time.

mcqs.question: If T(n) = T(n/2) + O(1), what is the complexity by Master Theorem?
mcqs.explanation: Using Master Theorem: a=1, b=2, f(n)=O(1). Here n^(log₂1) = n^0 = 1. This matches f(n), so complexity is O(log n).

mcqs.question: What is the auxiliary space complexity of Merge Sort?
mcqs.explanation: Merge Sort requires an auxiliary array of size n for merging, so its space complexity is O(n).

mcqs.question: Which notation represents tight bound?
mcqs.explanation: Big Theta (Θ) represents the tight bound, meaning the algorithm is both O(f(n)) and Ω(f(n)).

mcqs.question: What is the time complexity of accessing an element in an array by index?
mcqs.explanation: Array index access takes constant time regardless of array size because memory addresses are calculated directly.

mcqs.question: For recurrence T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n, what is the complexity?
mcqs.explanation: Using Master Theorem: a=2, b=2, f(n)=n. n^(log₂2) = n. Since f(n) = Θ(n), case 2 applies: T(n) = Θ(n log n).

mcqs.question: What is the relationship between O, Θ, and Ω notations?

options.text: If f(n) = Θ(g(n)), then f(n) = O(g(n)) and f(n) = Ω(g(n))

mcqs.explanation: By definition, Θ(g(n)) means the function is bounded both above and below by g(n), so it is both O(g(n)) and Ω(g(n)).

mcqs.question: Which complexity function grows fastest as n increases?
mcqs.explanation: Exponential (2^n) grows much faster than polynomial (n²) or linearithmic (n log n) functions as n increases.

mcqs.question: What is amortized analysis?
mcqs.data-1: Analyzing average cost per operation over a sequence of operations

options.text: Analyzing average cost per operation over a sequence of operations

mcqs.explanation: Amortized analysis gives the average performance per operation over a worst-case sequence, used for data structures like dynamic arrays.

mcqs.question: In the Master Theorem, if f(n) = O(n^(log_b a - ε)), what is the solution?
mcqs.explanation: Case 1 of Master Theorem: if f(n) = O(n^(log_b a - ε)), then T(n) = Θ(n^(log_b a)).

mcqs.question: What is the recurrence relation for Binary Search?
mcqs.explanation: Binary Search divides the problem into half each time with O(1) work at each level, giving T(n) = T(n/2) + O(1).

mcqs.question: Which sorting algorithm has worst-case O(n log n)?
mcqs.explanation: Merge Sort guarantees O(n log n) time complexity in all cases (best, average, worst).

mcqs.question: What does little o notation represent?
mcqs.explanation: Little o (o) represents an upper bound that is not tight - it indicates f(n) grows strictly slower than g(n).

mcqs.question: Space complexity of an in-place algorithm is:
mcqs.explanation: An in-place algorithm uses only O(1) additional space regardless of input size, using the input space itself for computation.
